title: Artificial Intelligence and the Future of Terrorism
gist: Byman and Subrahmanian argue AI will reinforce existing terrorism trends (lone actors, digital propaganda, disinformation) cumulatively rather than enabling revolutionary superweapons, while simultaneously supercharging state counterterrorism and surveillance, making the core challenge institutional and civil-liberties-related rather than purely technological.

insights.claim: AI is likely to reinforce existing trends in decentralization, lone-actor violence, digital propaganda, and information warfare rather than create entirely new forms of terrorism.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman and V.S. Subrahmanian
insights.so_what: Discounts catastrophic AI-terror narratives; the actionable risk surface is propaganda, fraud, and radicalization at the margin.
insights.quote: AI is likely to reinforce existing trends in decentralization, lone-actor violence, digital propaganda, and information warfare rather than create entirely new forms of terrorism.

insights.claim: Terrorist groups are conservative, resource-constrained adopters rather than technological innovators, and guns and bombs accounted for over 80 percent of all terrorist attacks in the last decade.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman and V.S. Subrahmanian
insights.so_what: Low-tech methods remain dominant; investors in counterterror AI should size the market to marginal uplift, not transformation.
insights.quote: guns and several types of bombs accounted for over 80 percent of all terrorist attacks in the last decade

insights.claim: Extremists will rely overwhelmingly on commercial models, open-source tools, and widely available platforms rather than internally developed frontier systems, making adoption cumulative and opportunistic.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman and V.S. Subrahmanian
insights.so_what: The threat vector is diffusion of commodity AI, not frontier-lab leakage, which reframes where safety controls matter.
insights.locator: This pattern is likely to continue with AI

insights.claim: As of May 26, 2026, Hugging Face hosted 368,944 text-generation models, 100,333 text-to-image models, 5,670 text-to-speech models, and 1,723 text-to-video models, any of which could be leveraged to build extremist chatbots with few guardrails.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman and V.S. Subrahmanian
insights.so_what: The open-model long tail makes guardrails on frontier labs insufficient; regulation of model-sharing hubs is the gap.

insights.claim: Generative AI dramatically cuts the cost of persuasive multilingual propaganda, and AI translation is especially valuable for transnational movements spreading grievances across cultures.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman and V.S. Subrahmanian
insights.so_what: Propaganda cost-collapse is the most immediate operational uplift; content-moderation AI demand rises in tandem.

insights.claim: The Islamic State in Khorasan Province has run AI training courses for its propaganda arm since 2023 and created deepfake news bulletins, including a synthetic anchorman after the March 2024 Crocus City Hall attack.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman and V.S. Subrahmanian
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence deepfake propaganda is operational now, not hypothetical, raising urgency for detection tooling.

insights.claim: Social platforms substantially outmatch terrorists on AI, with Meta claiming billions invested and nearly 40,000 people working on safety and security, so most extremist propaganda still fails to reach audiences.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman and V.S. Subrahmanian
insights.so_what: The defender-advantage on major platforms caps propaganda efficacy, tempering the bull case for AI-terror threat inflation.
insights.locator: At the same time, the effects of AI-enabled propaganda

insights.claim: AI chatbots functioning as recruiters, mentors, or emotional companions could radicalize socially isolated lone actors, who are the most persistent threat in the U.S. context, though most isolated individuals never become terrorists.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman and V.S. Subrahmanian
insights.so_what: Companion-AI radicalization is the novel social vector; relevant to platform-safety liability and design regulation.

insights.claim: A May 2026 India National Investigation Agency charge sheet on a November 2025 Delhi bombing reportedly notes the accused, linked to al Qaeda in the Indian Subcontinent, used YouTube and ChatGPT to learn how to make a rocket-type IED and its mixture proportions.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman and V.S. Subrahmanian
entities: al Qaeda in the Indian Subcontinent
insights.so_what: A documented case of chatbot-assisted bomb-making that will drive real-world pressure for LLM guardrail liability.
insights.locator: For inexperienced actors, AI can greatly reduce

insights.claim: AI's operational effect will be evolutionary, producing a modest increase in the competence of lower-level actors rather than a surge in sophisticated attacks, because violence still requires trust, tradecraft, financing, and organizational infrastructure AI cannot supply.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman and V.S. Subrahmanian
insights.so_what: Sizes the operational threat as marginal uplift for lone actors, not a step-change in mass-casualty capability.
insights.locator: The likely result is therefore not a dramatic increase

insights.claim: AI models like AlphaFold that predict protein folding have few guardrails preventing malicious actors from designing pathogens, but tacit knowledge, lab infrastructure, and material acquisition remain major constraints on weaponization.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman and V.S. Subrahmanian
insights.so_what: Bio-design tools lower one barrier but not the physical ones; red-teaming protein models is the targeted policy lever.
insights.locator: A separate concern is the use of AI models such as AlphaFold

insights.claim: The bigger CBRN danger comes not from politically motivated groups with constituents but from nihilistic individuals and cults seeking mass casualties, a category the FBI now labels 'nihilistic violent extremists.'
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman and V.S. Subrahmanian
insights.so_what: Reframes bio-terror risk toward constituency-less actors, guiding where detection resources should concentrate.
insights.locator: A bigger danger comes from nihilistic individuals

insights.claim: Iran may exploit commercial AI to support proxy warfare through influence operations, cyber disruption, and open-source-intelligence analysis, though escalation concerns push state sponsors toward deniable, calibrated actions rather than catastrophic attacks.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman and V.S. Subrahmanian
insights.so_what: State-enabled AI proxy activity concentrates in cyber and influence, the domains where allied defenses and vendors matter most.
insights.locator: Iran, State Support, and AI-Enabled Proxy Activity

insights.claim: The same AI empowering extremists will strengthen state counterterrorism via pattern recognition, financial-transaction analysis, call transcription and threat-scoring, and real-time vehicle tracking from drone and satellite imagery.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman and V.S. Subrahmanian
insights.so_what: Government CT demand is the durable AI market here, spanning surveillance analytics, transcription, and computer vision vendors.

insights.claim: The greatest long-term danger may be the misuse of AI in the name of counterterrorism, as automation bias and expansive surveillance architectures could evolve into systems of political monitoring and social control.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman and V.S. Subrahmanian
insights.so_what: The authors flag surveillance overreach, not terrorist capability, as the central democratic risk, shaping oversight regulation.
insights.quote: one of the greatest long-term dangers may be the misuse of AI in the name of counterterrorism
insights.locator: There is also a broader danger

insights.claim: Byman and Subrahmanian urge independent auditing of counterterrorism AI claims, institutionalized public-private coordination, red-team testing of chemical and biological AI capabilities, and robust civil-liberties protections rather than reliance on voluntary corporate assurances.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman and V.S. Subrahmanian
insights.so_what: Points to a compliance regime of independent AI audits and red-teaming that would create demand for third-party evaluators.

top_entities: al Qaeda in the Indian Subcontinent
